coredata.dc:title: Functional nanocellulose hydrogel with amino acid integration for enhanced Li/Fe separation in LiFePO4 batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Hanmeng
Zhao, Zhimin
Wang, Chenguang
Gao, Hongshuai
Nie, Yi
coredata.dc:description: With the rising prevalence of lithium-ion batteries, efficient recovery of metal ions, particularly those potentially released from LiFePO4 anodes, has become critical. Given that both Fe3+ and Li+ ions can form electrostatic adsorptive interactions, achieving effective separation of conventional adsorbent materials becomes challenging. This study presents an amino acid-functionalized nanocellulose hydrogel (ANH) synthesized by incorporating L-threonine, which significantly enhances the selective adsorption of Fe3+ in a mixed-ion environment by leveraging coordination differences between Li+ and Fe3+. The morphology, functional groups, and pore structure of ANH were extensively characterized using scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and mercury intrusion porosimetry techniques. Through batch experiments, the adsorption thermodynamics and isotherms of ANH for Fe3+ were examined. Furthermore, the adsorption selectivity of ANH for Li+ and Fe3+ was evaluated in a mixed-ion system, revealing that the adsorption capacity for Fe3+ was four times higher than for Li+ at elevated concentrations. The adsorption mechanism of Li+/Fe3+ was elucidated through multi-scale simulations, and the influence of varying amino acid grafting degree on adsorption metrics, such as solvent-accessible area and hydrogen bonding numbers, was investigated. The combination of experimental and theoretical results demonstrates the potential of ANH to inform the development of high-performance, selective adsorbents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocellulose
Hydrogel
Li+/Fe3+ separation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Henan Province [grant number 242300421037 ]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 22278398 , 22108283 ]; and the Major Science and Technology Project of Henan Province [grant number 221100230700 ]. Sincerely appreciate Prof. Suojiang Zhang (IPE, CAS) for his careful academic guidance and great support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of samples. (a) TEM image of CNs, (b) SEM images of ANH, (c) FTIR spectra of L-threonine, CNs, and ANH, (d) XRD patterns of L-threonine, CNs, and ANH, (e) Thermogravimetric analysis of CNs and ANH, (f) High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of ANH, (g) Mercury intrusion and extrusion curves of ANH, (h) Pore size distribution curves of ANH.

(a) Saturated adsorption capacity of ANH for Li+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Mn2+, (b) visual comparison of ANH before and after Fe3+ adsorption, Effect of (c) pH and (d-e) temperature on adsorption performance, (f) Langmuir and (g) Freundlich adsorption isotherm models fitted to the Fe3+ adsorption data, (h) competitive adsorption performance of ANH for Li+ and Fe3+ at varying concentrations, (i) time-dependent adsorption capacity of ANH for Li+ and Fe3+, (j) adsorption cycling performance of ANH for Fe3+ in the presence of Li+.

SEM images of ANH before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption at (a) 25 μm and (b) 5 μm, (c) FTIR spectrum and (d) XPS spectra of ANH before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption, High-resolution (e) N1s and (f) O1s XPS spectra before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption.

(a) Electrostatic surface potential (ESP) of Li(H2O)+, Fe(H2O)6 3+, CNs, and ANH, Adsorption-optimized conformations of (b) CNs and (c) ANH with Li(H2O)+, Li(H2O)2 +, Li(H2O)3 +, and Fe(H2O)6 3+, visualization of the IGMH interaction region for (d) CNs and (e) ANH with Li(H2O)+, Li(H2O)2 +, Li(H2O)3 +, and Fe(H2O)6 3+.

(a-d) Radial distribution function (RDF) of CNs (O) and ANH (O) with Li+/Fe3+, (e) optimized adsorption configurations for CNs with 2Li+, 2Fe3+, and mixed Li+/Fe3+, (f) optimized adsorption configurations for ANH with 2Li+, 2Fe3+, and mixed Li+/Fe3+, (g) effect of various grafting degrees on solvent-accessible surface area (SASA) and gyration radius (Gyrate), (h) effect of different grafting degrees on the number of hydrogen bonds within molecules and between molecules and solvents.
Thermodynamic parameters of ANH for Fe3+ adsorption.

head.ce:title: Functional nanocellulose hydrogel with amino acid integration for enhanced Li/Fe separation in LiFePO4 batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

State Key Laboratory of Mesoscience and Engineering
CAS Key Laboratory of Green Process and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Longzihu New Energy Laboratory, Henan University, Kaifeng 475001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Longzihu New Energy Laboratory, Henan University, Kaifeng 475001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: 1 North 2nd Street, Zhongguancun, Haidian District, 100190 Beijing, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rising prevalence of lithium-ion batteries, efficient recovery of metal ions, particularly those potentially released from LiFePO4 anodes, has become critical. Given that both Fe3+ and Li+ ions can form electrostatic adsorptive interactions, achieving effective separation of conventional adsorbent materials becomes challenging. This study presents an amino acid-functionalized nanocellulose hydrogel (ANH) synthesized by incorporating L-threonine, which significantly enhances the selective adsorption of Fe3+ in a mixed-ion environment by leveraging coordination differences between Li+ and Fe3+. The morphology, functional groups, and pore structure of ANH were extensively characterized using scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and mercury intrusion porosimetry techniques. Through batch experiments, the adsorption thermodynamics and isotherms of ANH for Fe3+ were examined. Furthermore, the adsorption selectivity of ANH for Li+ and Fe3+ was evaluated in a mixed-ion system, revealing that the adsorption capacity for Fe3+ was four times higher than for Li+ at elevated concentrations. The adsorption mechanism of Li+/Fe3+ was elucidated through multi-scale simulations, and the influence of varying amino acid grafting degree on adsorption metrics, such as solvent-accessible area and hydrogen bonding numbers, was investigated. The combination of experimental and theoretical results demonstrates the potential of ANH to inform the development of high-performance, selective adsorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used in portable communication devices, smart wearables, and the aerospace sector, owing to their high energy density, extended service life, and lack of memory effect [1–3]. Among commercially available LIBs, common cathode materials include LiCoO2, LiFePO4 (LFP), LiMn2O4, and ternary systems [4]. Notably, LFP batteries constitute over 30 % of the LIB market, owing to their advantages in cycle stability, cost-efficiency, and safety [5,6]. As the demand for LFP batteries rises, the volume of used batteries will inevitably increase, intensifying the need for efficient recovery processes. Simultaneously, the global lithium supply is constrained, and rising lithium salt prices underscore the importance of reclaiming lithium, a valuable metal in LFP batteries, to mitigate future supply challenges. The current LFP battery recycling process typically involves three primary steps: (1) pre-treatment of used batteries, including discharge, dismantling, crushing, and screening; (2) separation of electrode materials from collectors through organic solvents or acid and alkali leaching; and (3) extraction and separation of valuable metals [7,8]. The recovery of lithium and iron from the cathode material is a pivotal and complex step in LFP battery recycling. Present methods for recovery include chemical precipitation, solvent extraction, adsorption, chemical redox method as well as electrochemical and biochemical approaches [9–13]. Among these, adsorption is particularly promising due to its non-destructive nature, low energy consumption, operational simplicity, and environmental friendliness, making it an appealing technique for the selective separation of lithium and iron in battery systems.

The success of adsorption techniques depends critically on the choice of adsorbent. Given this context, the design and development of a highly efficient, selective adsorbent for Li+ and Fe3+ ions is essential. Cellulose, a naturally abundant and degradable material, offers substantial potential as an adsorbent. Constituting 40 %–50 % of wood, cellulose is a polyhydroxylated natural polymer with hydrophilic properties, enabling diverse chemical modifications [14,15]. This versatility has attracted significant interest in cellulose as a matrix for biomass-based adsorbents [16–18]. To enhance recyclability, cellulose is often fabricated into macroscopically structured materials such as microspheres, films, sponges, and hydrogels [19–22]. Among these, cellulose-based hydrogels are particularly advantageous due to their three-dimensional porous structure, which can incorporate functional substances to enhance adsorption capacity. In addition, natural amino acids are readily available, affordable, biocompatible, and environmentally friendly [23,24]. These molecules, containing both amino and carboxyl groups, exhibit amphipathic properties that facilitate diverse intermolecular non-covalent interactions, including ionic, salt-bridge, and coordination bonds. This unique chemical diversity provides substantial scope for developing co-assembled adsorbent materials based on amino acid monomers integrated with cellulose substrates.

An effective adsorbent must exhibit both high adsorption capacity for metal ions and selectivity for Li+/Fe3+ ions. Therefore, our research emphasizes designing materials with enhanced selectivity in the development of adsorbents. Computational model-based molecular engineering can predict suitable adsorbent materials by calculating fundamental vibrational frequencies and zero-point energy, thereby reducing trial-and-error failures. Additionally, simulations offer valuable insights into the interactions between adsorbents and metal ions on a molecular level. Density functional theory (DFT) serves as a powerful tool for determining electrostatic potentials, identifying multiple adsorption sites, and calculating the binding energies of hydrated metal ions and adsorbents [25–27]. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation further elucidates molecular interaction, adsorption behavior, and transport mechanism at a molecular scale [28,29]. Based on these approaches, we explore the feasibility of creating co-assembled hydrogels combining nanocellulose and amino acid monomers for Li+/Fe3+ ions separation using both experimental and computational simulations.

In this study, an amino acid-functionalized nanocellulose hydrogel (ANH) was synthesized by introducing amino acids, and its application for Li+/Fe3+ separation in LFP battery systems was evaluated. The novel adsorbent was characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and mercury intrusion porosimetry (MIP) techniques. Batch experiments were conducted to examine the effects of pH, temperature, and metal ion concentration on adsorption performance. In addition, we investigated the selective adsorption behavior of ANH on Li+/Fe3+ in a mixed system. To understand the selective adsorption mechanism in depth, we analyzed ANH's pore size, functional groups, and adsorption sites before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption using SEM, FTIR, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and MIP. Multiscale simulations (DFT and MD) were employed to examine adsorption binding energies and the impact of varying degrees of amino acid grafting on ANH's adsorption properties before and after adsorption. Results demonstrate that functionalized nanocellulose-based hydrogels can efficiently separate Li+/Fe3+ in LFP battery systems, providing a robust foundation for the future recovery of Li+.

NaBr, NaClO, NaOH, 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), L-threonine (C4H9NO3), acrylic acid (AA), N′, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), potassium persulfate (PPS), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were sourced from Aladdin Corporation, China. All materials were analytical reagent and do not require further purification.

The synthesis of cellulose nanocrystals (CNs) begins with bleached wood pulp, which undergoes a series of chemical treatments to reduce its color. Initially, the wood pulp is treated with chlorine, followed by washing with sodium hydroxide to remove lignin fragments generated during chlorination. After multiple washings, the wood pulp is further treated with sodium hypochlorite, washed again with sodium hydroxide, and finally treated once more with hypochlorite. The resulting bleached wood pulp is primarily composed of cellulose, with nearly all other components, including pectin, lignin, and hemicellulose, effectively removed.

CNs were synthesized using established methods described in the literature [30]. Initially, bleached wood pulp was dispersed uniformly in deionized water. Next, 0.16 g of TEMPO, 0.8 g of NaBr, and 12 g of NaClO were thoroughly combined, and the oxidation reaction was conducted over 7 h. The resulting mixture was neutralized and then passed through a microfluidizer six times to obtain CNs. The resulting CNs were stored in refrigeration for subsequent applications.

The specific synthesis steps of ANH are as follows: A 16 g CNs suspension (CNs:H2O = 1:3) was prepared, followed by the addition of 1 g of AA, and sonicated for 10 min. After complete dissolution, L-threonine was added to achieve CNs-to-L-threonine mass ratios of 1:2, 1:4, 1:6, 1:8, and 1:10. Subsequently, 0.15 g of MBA and 0.5 g of PPS were introduced, and the mixture was sonicated for 30 min to ensure homogeneity and remove air bubbles. The solution was then reacted at 70 °C for 1 h to obtain hydrogels. The hydrogels were named ANH-1, ANH-2, ANH-3, ANH-4, and ANH-5 according to the respective mass ratios.

The morphology of ANH was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). FTIR spectra of CNs, L-threonine, and ANH were recorded with an FTIR spectrometer (American Thermal Power Company, USA) in attenuated total reflectance mode. XRD spectra for CNs, L-threonine, and ANH were obtained using a Rigaku spectrometer (Rigaku Co., Ltd., Japan). Thermal stability of CNs and ANH was examined using a thermogravimetry–differential thermal analyzer (Hitachi, Japan). MIP (Micromeritics, USA) provided data on ANH's pore size distribution. XPS spectra of ANH, before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption, were collected using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. The equilibrium concentration of ANH after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption was measured with an ICPE-9000 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The adsorption selectivity of ANH for metal cations in LiFePO4 and Li(NiCoMn)O2 batteries was evaluated. Approximately 5 g of ANH was introduced into solutions containing various metal ions (Li+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Mn2+) at an initial concentration of 2000 mg L−1 for 48 h. The equilibrium concentrations post-adsorption were measured with an ICPE-9000 spectrometer. Comparative analysis of adsorption selectivity across battery systems indicated that ANH exhibited the highest adsorption performance for Fe3+ in LiFePO4 batteries. Subsequently, the effects of pH and temperature on Fe3+ adsorption by ANH were investigated. Given Fe3+'s tendency toward hydrolysis, the pH was maintained below 3 (specifically at pH = 1, 1.5, 2, and 2.5), with an adsorption duration of 48 h. Temperature effects were assessed across five gradients (25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C, 55 °C, and 65 °C), with adsorption time reduced to 10 h to conserve energy. The concentration of Fe3+ solutions in these tests was uniformly set at 200 mg L−1. To further explore ANH's adsorption thermodynamics, the equilibrium distribution coefficient was determined over the temperature range of 298.15–338.15 K. Thermodynamic parameters, including enthalpy (ΔH°, kJ·mol−1), entropy (ΔS°, J·mol−1·K−1) and Gibbs free energy (ΔG°, kJ·mol−1), were then calculated from the slope and intercept of the Van't Hoff plot. The equations used are as follows [31–33]:
(1)
K d
=
C 0
−
C e
C e
×
V m
(2)
lnK d
=
Δ
S °
R
−
Δ
H °
RT
(3)
Δ
G °
= Δ
H °
− T Δ
S °
where C 0 and C
e represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of metal ions solution, respectively, and m and V are the absolute mass of ANH and the volume of metal ions solution, respectively. The value of K d is mainly obtained through Eq. (1). T denotes the temperature (K), and R is the ideal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1).

Additionally, the adsorption isotherms of ANH were explored at elevated Fe3+ concentrations (200, 400, 800, 1200, 1600, 2000, 2400, 2800, and 3200 mg L−1). To further evaluate ANH's adsorption selectivity for Li+ and Fe3+ in LiFePO4 batteries, the adsorption capacities of ANH for each ion were compared at varying concentrations (200, 400, 800, 1200, and 1600 mg L−1). The competitive adsorption between Li+ and Fe3+ was also examined by introducing ANH into mixed Li+ and Fe3+ solutions, sampled at intervals (10, 20, 40, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300, 360, and 420 min) with both ions at 200 mg L−1. The adsorption capacity of ANH after 4 cycles of Fe3+ adsorption and desorption was evaluated using a 0.1 mol L−1 HCl solution as the desorption agent. Additionally, the adsorption capacity of ANH in actual LFP battery wastewater (Langfang, China) was investigated. Notably, the recovery process of Li+ from the solution involves the following steps: following the adsorption of Fe3+ from the mixed solution, sodium carbonate is added until the pH exceeds 10. The solution is then filtered, and excess sodium carbonate is washed away with distilled water. Finally, the solution is evaporated and crystallized to yield the recovered lithium carbonate. The metal ion concentrations in all adsorption experiments were quantified using an ICPE-9000 spectrometer.

To improve the efficiency of the DFT simulations and enhance the realism of the results, the calculated monomers representing the performance of ANH were shown in Fig. 1 . All DFT calculations were performed using GAUSSIAN 16C.01 software [34]. Geometry optimization was conducted with the B3LYP functional [35,36], incorporating Grimme's D3 version dispersion correction [37]. The 6-31G* and SDD basis sets [38] were applied for main group elements (carbon, nitrogen, lithium, oxygen, and hydrogen) and the transition metal element Fe, respectively. Solvent effects were approximated using the SMD water model [39]. Electrostatic surface potentials (ESP) were calculated with Multiwfn 3.8 [40] and visualized via VMD 1.9.3 [41]. The adsorption energy was determined using the following equation:
(4)
E ads
=
E total
−
E A
−
E B
where E
total denotes the energy of the adsorption composite structure, A is the computational monomer of ANH, and B is the cluster of Li+/Fe3+–water.

MD simulations were conducted using GROMACS 2019.6 software [42]. First, CNs with a polymerization degree of 20 were constructed. Amino acid modifications were applied to CNs with degrees of modification set to 0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %. To analyze the MD behavior of CNs and ANH in pure water and metal ion solutions, periodic cubic boxes with dimensions of 10 nm × 10 nm × 10 nm were established. Once an appropriate model was achieved, we assigned the RESP charge model [43] and the OPLS-AA force field parameters [44] to all atoms within the CNs and ANH. For the MD simulations, energy minimization was performed using the steepest descent method to alleviate internal stress within the model. Next, the CNs and ANH structures were fixed and simulated for 1 ns within the NVT ensemble. Finally, a 100 ns production run was performed under NPT ensemble conditions at 300 K and 1 bar. The resulting data were subsequently analyzed for insights into adsorption behaviors and interactions.

The synthesis of ANH is illustrated in Fig. 1. CNs were first prepared from bleached wood pulp through TEMPO-mediated oxidation. Different proportions of L-threonine, AA, and MBA were then thoroughly mixed and sonicated. After complete dissolution, PPS was introduced, and the reaction was carried out at 70 °C. The resulting ANH exhibited a porous structure with abundant adsorption sites. Adsorption properties were compared for ANHs with varying CNs-to-L-threonine ratios (ANH-1–ANH-5). As shown in Fig. S1, ANH-3 demonstrated the highest adsorption capacity for Fe3+, and thus was selected for subsequent characterization and testing.

The TEM image of CNs and the SEM images of ANH are presented in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. The porous architecture of ANH was successfully observed in Fig. 2b. SEM images reveal a smooth surface of ANH. Fig. 2c shows the FTIR spectra of L-threonine, CNs, and ANH. In L-threonine, the characteristic peak at 1620 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric bending of the amino group [45,46], while the band at 1420 cm−1 is assigned to symmetric stretching of the COO− group [47]. The spectral peak at 1110 cm−1 indicates NH3 [46,48], and a peak at 1040 cm−1 is attributed to CN stretching in the amino group [47]. The wagging vibration of CO2 − is observed at 699 cm−1 [47,48]. For CNs, characteristic peaks at 3342 and 2915 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of –OH and –CH2 groups, respectively [49], with a notable peak at 1057 cm−1 confirming the cellulose structure. In ANH, a new peak at 1710 cm−1 is observed, attributed to the –COO− group, while the peak at 1540 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of -NH2 [50].

Fig. 2d presents the XRD spectra of L-threonine, CNs, and ANH. The diffraction peaks of L-threonine are observed at 13.3°, 17.5°, 22.8°, 26.3°, 28.8°, 30.5°, 35.2°, and 46.5° [51]. For CNs, characteristic peaks associated with cellulose type I appear at 15.1° and 22.6°. However, these peaks are barely visible in the ANH spectrum, suggesting that the polymerization between CNs, L-threonine, and AA disrupted the crystalline structure, resulting in the formation of an amorphous structure. Fig. 2e shows the thermal stability analysis of ANH and CNs, indicating that ANH remains stable up to 180 °C. This enhanced thermal stability is important for its application in varying temperature environments. Elemental composition was analyzed through XPS, with the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of ANH displayed in Fig. 2f. The peak at 284.80 eV corresponds to CC bonds, while the peak at 285.96 eV is attributed to CN bonds [52]. The peaks at 288.78 eV and 292.82 eV are assigned to the –CONH– and –COO− groups [52,53], respectively, confirming the successful grafting of amino acids onto the CNs. MIP was employed to assess the pore structure of ANH. Fig. 2g shows the mercury intrusion and extrusion curves, from which the porous structure parameters were derived. Fig. 2h displays the pore size distribution of ANH, indicating that the material is predominantly macroporous, with a lack of mesopores and micropores. This macroporous structure facilitates broad adsorption channels, providing ample exposure to adsorption sites, which enhances the material's capacity for metal ion adsorption.

The adsorption behavior of ANH for several metal cations at elevated concentrations in LiFePO4 and Li(NiCoMn)O2 battery systems was investigated through batch experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the difference in the adsorption capacity of ANH for Li+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, and Mn2+ is obvious, indicating a notably higher adsorption capacity for Fe3+ over Li+ in the LiFePO4 system. This result highlights ANH's preferential adsorption for Fe3+, thus Fe3+ was chosen for further adsorption studies. Fig. 3b illustrates ANH's adsorption behavior for Fe3+. A cylindrical hydrogel sample was placed into a Fe3+ solution (200 mg L−1). Over time, Fe3+ ions were increasingly adsorbed onto the hydrogel surface, turning the adsorbent yellow. Upon reaching adsorption equilibrium, the Fe3+ solution transitioned from yellow to a clear, colorless state, indicating efficient Fe3+ adsorption by ANH.

The effects of pH and temperature on ANH's Fe3+ adsorption performance are shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. To prevent Fe3+ hydrolysis, the pH was maintained below 3 [54]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the adsorption capacity of Fe3+ increased with rising pH, potentially due to reduced protonation of adsorption sites like carboxyl groups at higher pH, enhancing Fe3+ binding [55]. Fig. 3d demonstrates that adsorption capacity also increased with temperature, indicating endothermic behavior. The corresponding thermodynamic parameters, calculated from eqs. (1–3), are presented in Table 1 .

The data in Table 1 reveal that the positive values of ΔH° and ΔS° suggest an endothermic and increasingly random adsorption process of ANH for Fe3+. The negative ΔG° value confirms the spontaneity of this adsorption. The adsorption isotherms of ANH were further examined in high-concentration Fe3+ solutions and fitted using both Langmuir (Fig. 3f) and Freundlich models (Fig. 3g). The Langmuir model yielded a theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of 292.58 mg g−1. Comparing the correlation coefficients (R 2) of these models, the Freundlich model showed a higher R 2 of 0.977 versus 0.931 for the Langmuir model, indicating that Fe3+ adsorption on ANH predominantly occurs on the adsorbent's heterogeneous surface [56].

The selective adsorption behavior of ANH toward Li+ and Fe3+ was also investigated. Fig. 3h shows that the adsorption capacities of ANH for both ions increase with metal ion concentration; however, Fe3+ adsorption consistently exceeds that of Li+. At a metal ion concentration of 1600 mg L−1, ANH's adsorption capacity for Fe3+ reaches 266.55 mg g−1, whereas for Li+ it remains at 57.96 mg g−1. Time-dependent adsorption performance was also evaluated, as shown in Fig. 3i. The adsorption capacity of ANH for both ions increases with time until equilibrium is reached, yet the equilibrium capacity for Fe3+ is approximately four times that of Li+, affirming ANH's high selectivity for Fe3+ over Li+. Finally, the adsorption recyclability of ANH for Fe3+ in the presence of Li+ was tested, using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl as the desorption agent. As shown in Fig. 3j, after four adsorption and desorption cycles, ANH's Fe3+ adsorption capacity decreased from an initial 57.24 mg g−1 to 41.59 mg g−1, retaining over 70 % of its original capacity. This indicates good recyclability of the synthesized adsorbent. Additionally, some comparisons of the maximum adsorption capacities of Fe3+ using other well-known materials were provided in Table S1.

SEM images of ANH before and after Li+ and Fe3+ adsorption at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 4a and b. Fig. 4a reveals significant structural changes in the pore architecture of ANH after adsorption of both ions. In the higher magnification image (Fig. 4b), the surface of ANH prior to adsorption appears smooth with abundant porosity, while after Li+ adsorption, the surface becomes rougher, and after Fe3+ adsorption, the pore size diminishes noticeably. For clarify these pore structure modifications, MIP was employed to measure changes in average pore diameter before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption. The mercury intrusion/extrusion curves and pore size distributions for ANH following Li+ adsorption are shown in Figs. S2 and S3, while those for Fe3+ adsorption are displayed in Figs. S4 and S5. Table S2 summarizes key pore structure parameters, including total intrusion volume, apparent skeletal density, and average pore diameter of ANH before and after adsorption. According to Table S2, ANH's apparent skeletal density increased from 0.34 to 0.92 g mL−1 after Li+ adsorption and further to 1.29 g mL−1 after Fe3+ adsorption, indicating selective and effective adsorption. Additionally, the average pore diameter of ANH decreased from 8.09 μm (before adsorption) to 2.04 μm after Li+ adsorption and to 0.27 μm after Fe3+ adsorption. These pore size reductions align with the structural changes observed in the SEM images, supporting the selective adsorption capability of ANH for Li+ and Fe3+ ions.

Fig. 4c presents the FTIR spectra of ANH before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption. Notably, the peak at 1710 cm−1, attributed to the –COO− group, remains largely unchanged following Li+ adsorption but shows a marked decrease in intensity after Fe3+ adsorption, suggesting that carboxyl functional groups play a significant role in Fe3+ adsorption [57]. Fig. 4d shows the XPS survey spectra of ANH before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption. The spectra reveal the presence of Li 1 s at 55.1 eV and Fe 2p at 711.1 eV, with the Fe 2p peak exhibiting greater intensity. This difference suggests selective adsorption of Fe3+ and Li+, indicative of ANH's preferential affinity for Fe3+ ions. The high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s and O 1 s before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption are illustrated in Fig. 4e and f, respectively. In Fig. 4e, the N 1 s spectrum of ANH initially displays a single peak at 399.67 eV, attributed to the NH group [58]. Post-adsorption, the N 1 s peak area decreases slightly, indicating that the NH group participates in the adsorption process. In Fig. 4f, the O 1s spectrum of ANH prior to adsorption can be fitted into two peaks at 531.81 and 533.21 eV, corresponding to CO and CO groups, respectively. After Li+/Fe3+ adsorption, both peaks shift slightly to lower binding energies, confirming the strong binding interaction between the carboxyl groups and the metal ions. Furthermore, the peak area at 533.21 eV decreases substantially following Fe3+ adsorption, while it remains relatively stable after Li+ adsorption, further demonstrating the site's selective affinity for Fe3+ over Li+.

To examine the selective adsorption behavior of ANH for Fe3+ and Li+ in solution, we used theoretical calculations to clarify the adsorption mechanism, with CNs as a comparison. Fig. 5a illustrates the ESP for Li(H2O)+, Fe(H2O)6 3+ clusters, CNs, and ANH's repeating unit. The metal ion clusters exhibit a net positive charge, with Fe(H2O)6 3+ clusters showing a higher positive charge and greater global electrostatic potential than Li(H2O)+ clusters, suggesting stronger electrostatic interactions and a higher adsorption capacity of ANH for Fe(H2O)6 3+ clusters.

For further explore the role of CNs and ANH in interactions during Li(H2O)+ and Fe(H2O)6 3+ adsorption, we analyzed binding energies and weak interaction regions (Fig. 5b–e). Fig. 5b and c displays the stabilized adsorption configurations of CNs and ANH with Li(H2O)ₙ+ (n = 1–3) and Fe(H2O)6 3+ clusters. The adsorption energy values of CNs/ANH with Li(H2O)n + and Fe(H2O)6 3+ cluster were displayed in Tables S3 and S4. Binding energy results show that Li+ coordination to water molecules minimally affects the binding energy (CNs–Li(H2O)ₙ+: −49.54 to −52.03 kcal mol−1; ANH–Li(H2O)ₙ+: −48.49 to −54.70 kcal mol−1), indicating primarily electrostatic adsorption. Conversely, Fe3+ exhibits a higher binding energy due to coordination with carboxyl or dual‑carbonyl group (CNs–Fe(H2O)6 3+: −52.30 kcal mol−1; ANH–Fe(H2O)6 3+: −124.29 kcal mol−1). This disparity in Fe3+ binding energies pre- and post-modification confirms enhanced coordination as a primary factor in selective adsorption. The interacting regions in the adsorption-stabilized model are depicted in Fig. 5d and e, with green representing van der Waals interactions and electrostatic interaction regions and purple indicating coordination interaction regions. ANH's adsorption region for Fe(H2O)6 3+ clusters is notably larger, further enhancing binding energy and selectivity for Fe3+.

We conducted MD simulations using GROMACS 2019.6 software to further explore ANH's selective adsorption mechanism for Li+ and Fe3+ ions, applying all-atom force fields for system parameterization. Fig. 6a–d presents the radial distribution function results, quantifying the distribution of O sites (in CNs and ANH) relative to metal ions in a mixed LiCl and FeCl3 solution. After reaching equilibrium (100 ns), the O sites in CNs displayed minimal selective adsorption between Li+ and Fe3+ ions (Fig. 6a–b). In contrast, Fe3+ ions showed a higher affinity for the O sites in ANH over Li+ ions (Fig. 6c–d), consistent with our experimental results.

Given the tendency for co-adsorption phenomena in mixed systems, we complemented the MD simulations with DFT calculations to determine binding energies for dual-metal ion adsorption (Fig. 6e, f, Table S5). Fig. 6e shows that in CNs, the carboxyl group serves as the primary adsorption site, with binding energies for different ion configurations as follows: CNs-2Li+ (−87.32 kcal mol−1), CNs-2Fe3+ (−93.68 kcal mol−1), and CNs–Li+/Fe3+ (−86.22 kcal mol−1). This indicates stable adsorption for each configuration in CNs. Fig. 6f reveals that in ANH, carboxyl and dual‑carbonyl group act as adsorption sites, with binding energies as follows: ANH-2Li+ (−79.27 kcal mol−1), ANH–Li+/Fe3+ (−92.79 kcal mol−1), and ANH-2Fe3+ (−142.38 kcal mol−1). This comparison demonstrates that the incorporation of amino acids into ANH enhances binding affinity for Fe3+, supporting the selectivity and stability of Fe3+ adsorption.

To investigate the influence of amino acid grafting levels on selective adsorption, MD simulations of ANH with varying grafting degrees (0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 %) were conducted (Figs. S6–S10). Fig. 6g illustrates the effect of grafting degree on the solvent-accessible surface area (SASA) and radius of gyration under consistent polymerization degrees. Fig. 6h shows the effect of grafting degree on the number of hydrogen bonds, both intra-molecularly and between ANH and the solvent. At a 50 % grafting level, the intramolecular hydrogen bonds decrease significantly, while hydrogen bonding between ANH and the solvent increases, creating a linear structure that maximizes the solvent-accessible area and the cellulose chain's cyclotron radius. This configuration exposes more adsorption sites, thereby enhancing Fe3+ removal efficiency. However, as the grafting degree increases beyond 50 %, adsorption capacity declines, likely due to self-assembled structures forming within the material (Figs. S11–S15). Based on these observations, Fig. 7 presents the selective adsorption mechanism of ANH before and after Li+/Fe3+ adsorption.

This study reports the synthesis of a functional nanocellulose hydrogel integrated with amino acids, demonstrating its efficient and selective adsorption of Li+/Fe3+ in LFP batteries. Characterization through SEM, FTIR, XRD, and MIP confirmed the successful formation and detailed physicochemical structure of ANH. Batch experiments provided insights into the adsorption thermodynamics and isotherms of ANH for Fe3+. Additionally, competitive adsorption studies in a mixed-ion system highlighted ANH's enhanced selectivity for Fe3+, with Fe3+ adsorption surpassing that of Li+ by more than four times. Finally, the adsorption ratio of Fe3+ and the recovery rate of Li+ in actual LFP battery wastewater treated with ANH were quantified, yielding values of 96.9 % and 78.8 %, respectively. To further elucidate the adsorption selectivity mechanism, we employed experimental analyses and simulation calculations. DFT results indicated that ANH's high selectivity for Fe3+ is largely attributable to carboxyl or dual‑carbonyl group coordination sites formed by amino acids and nanocellulose, yielding a notable binding energy for Fe3+ (−124.29 kcal mol−1). The impact of amino acid grafting degree on ANH's adsorption efficacy was also assessed. Findings from SASA and hydrogen bonding analyses indicated that, at 50 % amino acid grafting, ANH exhibited the maximum SASA and highest hydrogen bonding count, enhancing Fe3+ adsorption. In summary, this work not only offers a novel approach for the design and synthesis of chemically modified polymer adsorbents but also introduces a promising strategy for efficient Li+ recovery in lithium iron phosphate battery systems.
Hanmeng Yuan: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhimin Zhao: Software. Chenguang Wang: Software. Hongshuai Gao: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yi Nie: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Henan Province [grant number 242300421037]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 22278398, 22108283]; and the Major Science and Technology Project of Henan Province [grant number 221100230700]. Sincerely appreciate Prof. Suojiang Zhang (IPE, CAS) for his careful academic guidance and great support.
